title: Inside the Brain of a 319-Million-Year-Old Fossil Fish
gist: A study in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences describes a 319-million-year-old fossil of the ray-finned fish Trawdenia planti from UK Carboniferous coalfield deposits, notable for preserving soft brain tissue that — unlike other fossilized early-fish brains — fits snugly inside its bony braincase, offering a rare data point on how ray-finned fishes' brains evolved after they inherited Earth's oceans from extinct armored placoderms.

insights.claim: Heavily armored placoderm fishes dominated Earth's oceans in the Devonian Period (roughly 400 million years ago) until a mass extinction at the end of the Devonian wiped them out, opening the ecological space that let ray-finned fishes (class Actinopterygii) — now the large majority of fish species alive today — diversify and flourish.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Frames the mass-extinction-driven niche release as the causal starting point for modern fish diversity, a reference case for how extinction events reset dominance hierarchies in an ecosystem.
insights.locator: opening paragraph, 'Most fish on Earth today'

insights.claim: A study published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences describes a fossil of the ray-finned fish Trawdenia planti recovered from 319-million-year-old (Carboniferous Period) coalfield deposits in the United Kingdom.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
entities: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
insights.so_what: Establishes the primary source and dating for the finding, letting readers trace the underlying peer-reviewed study.
insights.locator: 'a study published today in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences'

insights.claim: The Trawdenia planti fossil preserved ancient soft brain tissue, a type of tissue much less likely to fossilize than bone, making it an unusually direct window into early ray-finned fish neuroanatomy.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Soft-tissue brain fossils are rare enough that each new specimen materially expands the evidence base for early vertebrate brain evolution.
insights.locator: 'it included the ancient soft tissue of the fish's brain'

insights.claim: Unlike previously sampled fossilized brains from early fishes, which were much smaller than the bony endocranial cavity that housed them, the Trawdenia planti brain fit snugly inside its endocranium, mirroring the cavity's shape.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A tight brain-to-braincase fit in this specimen is the specific anatomical anomaly driving interest in the find, since it differs from the pattern seen in other early-fish brain fossils.
insights.locator: 'Unlike the sample of other fossilized brains from early fishes'

top_entities: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
